flashcards.back: A memory management technique where each process is allocated a single continuous block of memory addresses. The entire process occupies consecutive memory locations from a base address.

flashcards.front: Define Fixed Partitioning in memory management.
flashcards.back: Memory is divided into fixed-size partitions at system startup. Each partition can hold one process. Partitions can be equal-sized or unequal-sized.
Memory partitions are created dynamically based on the exact size of each process. Partition sizes vary according to the memory requirements of incoming processes.
Wasted memory within an allocated partition. Occurs when the partition is larger than the process requirements. The unused memory inside the partition cannot be used by other processes.
Total memory exists to satisfy a request, but it is not contiguous. Small holes of free memory are scattered throughout memory, making it impossible to allocate a large process.
A technique to combat external fragmentation by moving all free memory holes together to form one large contiguous block of free memory. All processes are relocated to make this possible.

flashcards.front: Explain the First Fit allocation strategy.
flashcards.back: Scans memory from the beginning and allocates the first hole (free block) that is large enough to accommodate the process. Fastest allocation method.

flashcards.front: Explain the Best Fit allocation strategy.
flashcards.back: Allocates the smallest hole that is large enough to fit the process. Results in smallest leftover fragment but requires scanning entire memory list.

flashcards.front: Explain the Worst Fit allocation strategy.
flashcards.back: Allocates the largest hole available to the process. Creates the largest leftover fragment, increasing chances of fitting future processes.

flashcards.front: What is the role of Base Register in memory protection?
flashcards.back: Base register stores the starting physical address of a process's memory partition. Used with limit register to check if memory accesses are within bounds.

flashcards.front: What is the role of Limit Register in memory protection?
flashcards.back: Limit register stores the size (length) of the process's memory partition. Used to ensure memory addresses do not exceed the allocated partition boundaries.
The process of mapping symbolic addresses (logical) to physical memory addresses. Can occur at compile time, load time, or execution time.

flashcards.front: What is Swapping in contiguous memory allocation?
flashcards.back: The process of moving entire processes in and out of main memory. A process can be temporarily moved to secondary storage to make room for other processes.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between logical and physical address?
flashcards.back: Logical address is generated by CPU (relative address), while physical address is the actual location in memory. In contiguous allocation, logical address = base register value + offset.

flashcards.front: What is Next Fit allocation strategy?
flashcards.back: Similar to First Fit but starts searching from where the last allocation ended. Tends to distribute free holes evenly throughout memory.
